motion: Defence asks to show the defendant's good character.
reason: Relevant to credibility.

motion: Prosecution wants to introduce hearsay from a stranger.
reason: Hearsay is generally inadmissible.

motion: Defence objects: witness was never disclosed before trial.
reason: Surprise witnesses unfair.

motion: Prosecution seeks to show a ten-year-old parking ticket.
reason: Irrelevant and prejudicial.

motion: Defence wants the jury to visit the crime scene at midnight.
reason: Impractical and theatrical.

motion: Prosecution offers a properly authenticated document.
reason: Authentic evidence is admissible.

motion: Defence objects because the officer read the witness's diary aloud.
reason: Privacy violation without warrant.

motion: Prosecution wants to play a video with no chain of custody.
reason: Cannot verify authenticity.

motion: Defence asks to mention the alleged victim's unrelated lawsuit.
reason: Prejudicial and irrelevant.

motion: Prosecution calls an expert with recognised qualifications.
reason: Expert testimony allowed.

motion: Defence objects to a leading question on direct examination.
reason: Leading questions improper on direct.

motion: Prosecution wants to introduce a confession obtained without caution.
reason: Involuntary confession excluded.

motion: Defence asks to show the jury a cute dog photo unrelated to the case.
reason: Irrelevant and distracting.

motion: Prosecution offers DNA from a verified lab.
reason: Reliable scientific evidence.

motion: Defence objects: prosecutor is making arguments during questioning.
reason: Argumentative questioning not allowed.

motion: Prosecution wants to mention the defendant's previous acquittal.
reason: Prior acquittal cannot be used against them.

motion: Defence objects: witness is speculating about what the defendant was thinking.
reason: Witness may not state another's state of mind without foundation.

motion: Prosecution seeks to introduce a hearsay statement made by a dying victim.
reason: Dying declaration is an recognised hearsay exception.

motion: Defence asks to introduce evidence of the victim's violent reputation.
reason: Character of the victim generally not admissible without a self-defence claim.

motion: Prosecution objects: defence counsel is badgering the witness on cross.
reason: Harassing or repeatedly asked questions are improper.

motion: Defence wants to read a poem written by the defendant in prison.
reason: No relevance to guilt or innocence.

motion: Prosecution seeks to introduce business records kept in the regular course of operations.
reason: Business records are an established hearsay exception.

motion: Defence objects: the question calls for a narrative, not specific facts.
reason: Open-ended narrative questions are improper on direct.

motion: Prosecution wants to ask the jury to put themselves in the victim's shoes.
reason: Improper emotional appeal and golden-rule argument.

motion: Defence objects: the document is being introduced without a sponsoring witness.
reason: Documents require authentication by a competent witness.

motion: Prosecution wants the courtroom cleared because the witness is a minor.
reason: Court may close proceedings to protect a minor witness.

motion: Defence asks to impeach a witness with a prior felony conviction from twenty years ago.
reason: Stale convictions carry little probative value and are generally excluded.

motion: Prosecution offers fingerprint evidence from an accredited crime lab.
reason: Reliable and properly authenticated scientific evidence.

motion: Defence objects: opposing counsel is asking the witness to speculate.
reason: Witnesses must testify to facts, not guesswork.

motion: Prosecution wants to introduce a statement made by a co-conspirator during the plot.
reason: Co-conspirator statements are a recognised hearsay exception.

motion: Defence asks to introduce polygraph test results proving the defendant passed.
reason: Polygraph evidence is widely considered unreliable and inadmissible.

motion: Prosecution objects: defence counsel is whispering to their client during testimony.
reason: Coaching a witness mid-testimony is improper.

motion: Defence wants to call seventeen character witnesses who all say the same thing.
reason: Cumulative evidence may be excluded to avoid wasting the court's time.

motion: Prosecution seeks to introduce surveillance footage with a proper chain of custody.
reason: Authenticated footage with intact chain of custody is admissible.

motion: Defence objects: the witness is testifying about events they never personally observed.
reason: Witnesses must have personal knowledge of the matters they address.

motion: Prosecution asks the jury instruction include an adverse-inference charge because defence destroyed evidence.
reason: Spoliation of evidence supports an adverse-inference instruction.
